title: Using Network Connectors
text: Using Network Connectors The TCP and TLS network connectors are automatically available in Windows, Unix, and Linux Uniface installations. To make use of them, you only need to specify the connector you want to use in the path assignments used to connect to the desired Uniface Server. These paths always go via the Uniface Router, which starts a Uniface Server when a client requests it. Paths are defined in assignment files: The assignment file of the client specifies the paths to the Uniface Servers it needs to access. The assignment file of the Uniface Router identifies the Uniface Servers it can start and the commands and parameters used to start them. The assignment file of the Uniface Server defines the paths and options for the data or services it provides. For more information, see Path-to-Connector Assignments and Network Connection Strings . Configuring Network Paths and Connections The Uniface Router always represents the server in a client/server connection, and it controls one or more Uniface Servers. To configure the connection from the Uniface Router to the Uniface Server, you need to configure the Uniface Router. For more information, see Configuring the Uniface Router . You can use the following assignment settings to set network defaults: $DEFAULT_TCP_HOST —set the default host identifier of the local host. For more information, see $DEFAULT_TCP_HOST . $DEFAULT_NET —set the default network path for the application. For more information, see $DEFAULT_NET . $DNP —specify the default network path to the Uniface Router. For more information, see $DNP . $NET_TIMEOUT —set the maximum length of time that one or more specified network activities can continue. For more information, see $NET_TIMEOUT . $NET_FMT —specify the compression or conversion format used to return a hitlist from the server to the client. For more information, see $NET_FMT . You can use the USYS$TCP_PARAMS and USYS$TLS_PARAMS settings to configure the network connectors. For more information, see TCP Connector Options and TLS Connector Options . Choosing the Network Connector When deploying Uniface applications, you need to consider the level of security required for the connections between client applications, Uniface Routers, and Uniface Servers. The TCP connector provides basic data transport with no security, whereas the TLS network connector provides endpoint verification and an encrypted network connection between the Uniface clients, Uniface Routers, and Uniface Servers. For each path, you can decide which connector to use, depending on your security requirements. For example, the connection between a Uniface Router and Uniface Server running on the same host behind a firewall could use the TCP connector. Connections between a client and Uniface Router or Uniface Server could be configured to use the TLS connector. The TCP connector is the default connector used when no connector is specified.
